data.title: As Trump, Xi trade shots, 'buy tech on sell-off,' Wedbush says
data.description:
Amid the back and forth between President Trump and President Xi Jinping over rare earth metals, Wedbush Securities believes investors should “buy tech” on the sell-off. 

“After a relatively calm few months and improving relations between the US and China, this step up in tensions has created a white-knuckle moment for the markets, with tech stocks under major pressure today,” Wedbush analyst Dan Ives wrote in a note to clients. “As we have navigated this drama since April, we continue to believe the bark will likely be worse than bite this time around as cooler heads prevail. This is all a game of high stakes poker going on between the US and China in this AI Revolution, as we are also seeing more scrutiny in Beijing around Nvidia's (NASDAQ:NVDA) golden chips.” 

He continued: “These moments we view as buying opportunities to own the winners in semis, software, Big Tech, and the AI future as in our view these tensions will not bubble up into a much more tense time vs. the nervous period of time we saw in April. That said, our view of tech stocks remains firmly bullish, but we will continue to have moments like this that create some panic and nervousness among tech investors.”

Given that mindset, Ives mentioned the large tech companies, such as Nvidia, Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR), Meta (NASDAQ:META), Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) and Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) are companies that are benefiting from the AI Industrial Revolution.

“Sell-offs like today we encourage investors to buy the tech winners and not head for elevators despite this war of words between Trump and Xi,” Ives added.

data.title: Semiconductors will grind higher despite U.S.-China tensions – analyst
data.description:
The semiconductor (NASDAQ:SMH), (SOXX), (XSD) ecosystem will continue to grow despite recent trade tensions between the United States and China affecting chipmakers like Qualcomm (QCOM), said Joshua Buchalter, TD Cowen senior analyst.

In an interview by CNBC, the analyst suggested that many of the investigations and export restrictions are being used as negotiating tactics in a larger trade deal that could be announced in the coming months.

“There’s no way to spin it. Increased tech trade tensions between the U.S. and China is not good for the semiconductor index (NASDAQ:SMH), (SOXX), (XSD),” he said.

However, he downplayed concerns about the Qualcomm (QCOM) investigation, noting it relates to “a tuck in acquisition in their auto business” that likely has “very minimal contribution to their revenue today.”

Regarding Qualcomm (QCOM) specifically, Buchalter explained that while the company’s auto business represents about $4B in revenue, the bigger concern would be if restrictions expanded to their smartphone business. “We really think that’s unlikely. Qualcomm (QCOM) is heavily embedded in the smartphone market, and there aren’t obvious and easy replacements for the breadth of their products,” he said.

When discussing AI and valuations in the sector, Buchalter expressed optimism about companies like NVIDIA (NVDA), which he doesn’t consider overvalued despite its nearly $5T market cap.

“If I look at where NVIDIA (NVDA) is trading… at high 20s [multiple], low 30s multiple for the growth rates that they’re putting up and for how important they are to the global compute ecosystem, I actually don’t think it’s over it’s all that expensive,” he said.

The analyst pointed to three simultaneous growth factors driving the semiconductor industry: increasing users, growing complexity of applications and workloads, and expanding use cases for AI.

“I actually think the demand for compute and the gap between supply and demand of where we are today has actually grown over the last twelve months,” he concluded, suggesting that “without these external factors disrupting things, which are impossible for people to predict, I do think this ecosystem is going to continue to grind higher.”

data.title: Bets against utility stocks increase in September; Evergy most shorted
data.description:
Bets against Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU) rose in September compared to the end of August, with Evergy (EVRG) being the most shorted stock in the sector and Eversource Energy (ES) taking the spot for the least shorted.

Average short interest across S&P 500 utilities stocks was 2.95% of shares float at the end of September, up from 2.76% at the end of August.

The S&P 500’s Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU) was up over 20% so far this year, compared to the broader S&P500 market’s gain of 14.5% during the same period.

Stocks with the least and largest short positions

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

Evergy (EVRG) had 13.12 million shares sold short as of September, or 5.78% of shares float.

Alliant Energy (LNT) was the second-most shorted stock with short interest of 5.42%, followed by CenterPoint Energy (CNP) with 4.74%, PPL (PPL) with 4.61%, and Xcel Energy (XEL) with 4.58%.

Eversource Energy (ES) was the least shorted stock, with 4.67 million shares sold short, or 1.26% of the shares float, followed by PG&E (PCG) and NextEra Energy (NEE) with short interest of 1.53% and 1.58%, respectively.

Industry analysis

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

Water Utilities was the most shorted industry within the sector, with 3.88% short interest as of September end, which declined from 4.16% a month ago.

Independent Power & Renewable Electricity Producers stood at the second spot with 3.76% short interest as of the end of September, up from 3.52% at the end of August.

Multi utilities stocks took the third spot with 3.32% short interest as of September end, compared to 3.19% at the end of August.

Gas Utilities stocks took at the fourth spot with 3.19% short interest as of the end of September, decreased from 3.46% at the end of August.

Bets against the Electric Utilities sector as a whole have increased to 2.71% at the end of September, compared to 2.50% a month ago.

data.title: Braskem plunges as Citigroup downgrades to Neutral
data.description:
Shares of Braskem (NYSE:BAK) were down over 7% after Citigroup cut rating on the petrochemical company to Neutral from Buy, citing lower sales volume.

The stock was trading at $2.36 in afternoon trading on Friday.

The brokerage also cut PT to R$8 from R$11, saying it has downgraded the stock over low valuation and uncertainties on the company’s capital structure.

“We also weight our new rating on current market conditions and lack of perspectives on a petrochemicals spreads cycle improvement,” noted Citigroup analyst Gabriel Barra.
Barra also expects Braskem to post low EBITDA in the third quarter, mainly marked by the “challenging competitive dynamics and spreads that the company is facing, while the company may have lost some operational leverage due to the lower FX trend in the quarter.”

The Brazilian company is set to report third quarter earnings on November 10. Last month, Braskem said it has hired financial and legal advisors to find ways to improve its capital structure, as the company struggles with the effects of a prolonged downturn in Brazil's chemical industry that is expected to continue for several more years.

The company has also grappled with the 2023 collapse of a salt mine in Brazil's Alagoas state that cost it its investment grade status.Seeking Alpha analysts, Wall Street and Seeking Alpha’s Quant ratings are all neutral on the company and consider it a Hold.

The stock has lost nearly 39% so far this year, underperforming the about 15% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

data.title: Playing defense: Jefferies highlights four consumer stocks with minimal China risk
data.description:
Jefferies has its eyes on where investors can take shelter in the consumer sector. Analyst Randal Konik said the preference with China headlines back in focus is to consider companies with no inventory risk (TKO and PLNT) and/or little to no exposure to China production (SN and BIRK). Konik and his team lined up four defensive plays if the trade developments worsen.

TKO Group (NYSE:TKO): Jefferies noted the high-margin, low-CAPEX business with roughly 75% of revenues under contract and meaningful unrealized synergies is a defensive play insulated from tariff risk. "We anticipate continued strength in rights renewal trends following recent deals for WWE PLEs and UFC, along with additional upside as the company optimizes event-related revenue through sponsorships, site fees, and more efficient pricing," wrote Kokik. The expectation is for significant free cash flow generation and robust shareholder returns via buybacks and dividends.

Planet Fitness (NYSE:PLNT): The firm thinks Planet Fitness (NYSE:PLNT) benefits from category leadership in the highly fragmented fitness market, supported by strong secular tailwinds such as strength training trends, Gen Z/Alpha adoption, and affordability. "Comps are reaccelerating following membership fee hikes, while international expansion remains in early stages. With improving IRRs and a refreshed management team acting as catalysts, we expect unit growth to return to 200+ annual openings, which could drive a meaningful multiple re-rating," advised Konik.

SharkNinja (NYSE:SN): Konik said the company was quick to move production out of China, with roughly 95% of U.S. shipments relocated and full transition expected by the end of the year. "Distribution remains agile, enabling new product launches to pivot toward international markets during the transition. Growth is fueled by a strong product assortment and a disruptive innovation platform, supported by plans to enter two new categories annually with a highly visible pipeline. Significant whitespace exists globally, with further margin expansion opportunities ahead," highlighted Konik.

Birkenstock (NYSE:BIRK): The view is that with 100% of production and 96% of raw materials sourced in Europe, Birkenstock (NYSE:BIRK) is well-positioned. The company's vertical integration is seen as allowing for additional production efficiencies, vendor negotiations, and optimized product mix allocation across regions.

data.title: Short sellers' bets rose against S&P 500 financial stocks in September
data.description:
Average short interest across S&P 500 financial (NYSEARCA:XLF) stocks rose at the end of September, after 1-basis-point drop in August.
 
The short interest rose to 2.04% in September from 2.01% in August, with the number of short interest shares in S&P 500 financials sector rose to 999.22M shares, up from 986.12M in August.
 
The August short interest has been revised to 2.01% from 1.98% due to inclusion of Robinhood (HOOD) and exclusion of MarketAxess Holdings (MKTX) in S&P 500 Financials index. 
 
The S&P 500 financial index (SP500-40) was up 0.04% in the last month, trailing the 3.53% gain recorded by S&P 500.

Short interest, which could be an indicator of pessimism, calculates the number of shares sold against the company float. (Short Interest % = Number of Shares Sold Short ÷ Stock Float).

Most shorted stocks:

Erie Indemnity Company (ERIE) - 8.02% vs. 7.32% last month.

Apollo Global Management (APO) - 7.50% vs 7.41%.

Franklin Resources (BEN) - 7.22% vs 6.37%.

Robinhood Markets (HOOD) - 7.09% vs 3.91%.

Coinbase Global (COIN) - 6.48% vs 5.74%.

Least shorted stocks:

Chubb (CB) – 0.66% vs 0.84%.

Mastercard (MA) – 0.68% vs 0.72% last month.

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) - 0.94% vs 0.98%.  

Aon (AON) - 0.99% vs 0.88%.

JPMorgan (JPM) - 0.99% vs 0.97%.

Short Interest as per industry:

 

 

Capital Market was the most shorted industry, with a short interest of 2.58%, followed by financial services with a short interest of 2.28%.

Insurance was the least shorted industry at 1.63% against 1.57% in the prior month.

data.title: Market Voices: Trump slams China on rare earths, AstraZeneca, UPS
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks. 

  • President Trump slammed China for rolling out new export restrictions on rare earth elements, which are used in a wide range of technologies and devices. 

In a long post on Truth Social, Trump said China was becoming "very hostile," and that the new export controls "would make life difficult for virtually every country in the world, especially for China." 

"There is no way that China should be allowed to hold the World 'captive,' but that seems to have been their plan for quite some time, starting with the 'Magnets' and other Elements that they have quietly amassed into somewhat of a Monopoly position, a rather sinister and hostile move, to say the least," Trump said in his post.

Trump said he has not yet discussed the matter with China's President Xi, whom he's scheduled to meet with in two weeks, adding that "now there seems to be no reason to do so." 

In response to the new export controls, Trump said his administration was "calculating" a "massive" tariff increase on Chinese goods. "There are many other countermeasures that are, likewise, under serious consideration," Trump added.

The Trump administration has been particularly keen on reducing America's reliance on China for rare earths, with the U.S. government recently taking equity stakes in MP Materials (MP), Trilogy Metals (TMQ), and Lithium Americas (LAC).

  • U.K. drugmaker AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN) has reportedly reached a deal with the Trump administration that will help lower the costs of its drugs for American consumers. 

The White House is expected to announce the deal on Friday, according to Bloomberg.  

Last month, Pfizer (PFE) announced it had reached a deal with the Trump administration that includes offering many of its medications at discounted prices directly to consumers through a new government website called TrumpRx.gov. 

  • UPS (NYSE:UPS) said that it has begun "disposing" of some of the U.S-bound packages piling up in its warehouses that have been unable to clear the Trump administration's new customs requirements, according to NBC News. 

“Because of changes to U.S. import regulations, we are seeing many packages that are unable to clear customs due to missing or incomplete information about the shipment required for customs clearance,” UPS said in a statement to NBC.

UPS said that if it can't get the necessary information for customs, it has the option of sending the package back to the sender at their expense. In cases where "the customer does not respond and the package cannot be cleared for delivery, disposing of the shipment is in compliance with U.S. customs regulations."

"We continue to work to bridge the gap of understanding tied to the new requirements and, as always, remain committed to serving our customers," UPS added.

data.title: GAMCO Investors takes an activist stake in Golden Entertainment
data.description:
GAMCO Investors disclosed in an SEC filing that it holds 1,323,338 shares of Golden Entertainment (NASDAQ:GDEN) through various funds. The position is considered an activist stake because GAMCO noted that it could issue analyst reports, participate in interviews, or hold discussions with third parties, with Golden Entertainment (NASDAQ:GDEN) management, or with directors in which it may suggest or take a position regarding potential changes in the operations, management, or capital structure as a means of enhancing shareholder value. 

The position by GAMCO Investors represents a 5.06% stake in Golden Entertainment (NASDAQ:GDEN).

GAMCO Investors is a prominent American investment management firm based in Rye, New York, founded and led by Mario Gabelli. The firm holds positions in various companies, including Crane Company (CR), American Express (AXP), and Playa Hotels & Resorts (PLYA).

data.title: Levi's beats on earnings but gets pantsed by guidance
data.description:
Levi Strauss (NYSE:LEVI) reported another strong quarter with gains across regions, channels, segments, and categories. The company beat Wall Street’s expectations and raised its FY25 profit and organic net revenue guidance.

Unfortunately, the guidance tripped up LEVI’s expected trajectory, leaving the stock to limp into Friday’s close with a double-digit loss and end the week with a loss for the first time in four weeks.

“We think underlying demand trends remain very strong and management is simply being prudently conservative,” Needham’s Tom Nikic said, viewing Friday’s 12% drop as a buying opportunity.

A closer look at the guidance did indeed raise some red flags. First, fourth quarter revenue will look “optically worse” on tougher comparisons, leaving a gap between now and the first and second quarters of next year when Levi’s topline durability will be tested.

Second, the failure of the company to “more fulsomely” convert improved revenue trends into significant profit expansion could be amplified by tariff pressures, Morgan Stanley analyst Alex Straton said in her note to clients.

The “profitability flow-thru magnitude may have fallen short of higher buyside expectations,” Straton adds, noting the modest $0.02 increase to FY25 profit guidance.

Fourth quarter revenue growth is also likely to cause some investor concern as management now calls for organic revenue growth of just 1%, “marking a rapid deceleration,” said Wells Fargo’s Ike Boruchow.

And of course, tariffs will present a lingering headwind for companies who rely on overseas manufacturing. With the majority of their merchandise made in Vietnam and Indonesia (subject to 20% and 19% tariffs, respectively), Levi (NYSE:LEVI) may not have fully incorporated the higher expense into margin compression.

While the guidance was cited as cause for the stock’s dismal performance, pre-earnings valuation was also problematic.

“Shares can only rise so much on fundamental improvements,” said Seeking Alpha analyst Daniel Jones, investing group leader for Crude Value Insights.

“The valuation of the company is one reason why I’m downgrading it [to Hold from Buy],” Jones said, reflecting his view that any firm that relies on imported goods should be “approached cautiously.”

Agrees fellow Seeking Alpha analyst Quipus Capital.

Levi’s (NYSE:LEVI) lofty valuation is even more concerning when you consider that the denim trend is showing some exhaustion, the current consumer environment in the U.S. could weigh on store traffic, and “the fact that the company has rarely been able to surpass current operating margins” all justify profit-taking pressure off Levi’s (LEVI) recent 4-year high, Quipus Capital says.

data.title: U.S. stocks sink as renewed tariff concerns ignite volatility amid continued government shutdown
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages opened in the green, but reversed course on Friday as U.S. President Donald Trump said China has "become very hostile" sending letters to other countries wanting to "impose export controls."

This comes as the U.S. entered its tenth day of government shutdown, while a consumer sentiment survey came in unchanged, but better than expected.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1.8% in afternoon trade, while the Dow (DJI) was -1.2%. The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last -2.4% as tech stocks tumbled after Trump threatened with massive tariffs on China.

On a Truth Social post, Trump said, "We have been contacted by other Countries who are extremely angry at this great Trade hostility, which came out of nowhere. Our relationship with China over the past six months has been a very good one, thereby making this move on Trade an even more surprising one.”

“The China trade war is getting hot. The Chinese seem unhappy about peace in the Middle East as they’ve been using it to divide our nation on their TikTok platform. Last night they began to threaten rare earth exports crucial to the US. Trump is now looking at a tougher tariff stance,” said Ross Gerber, co-Founder, president and CEO of Gerber Kawasaki Wealth and Investment Management.

The University of Michigan's U.S. consumer sentiment survey showed sentiment barely slowing to 55.0, down from 55.1 in September, and better than the 54.1 expected. Also, the 1-year inflation expectation came in at 4.6%, vs. 4.7% consensus.

In addition, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics said on Friday that it will publish the September 2025 CPI report by the end of the month to provide the Federal Reserve with inflation data before its next meeting.

Over in the bond market, yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 8 basis points lower at 4.06%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 8 basis points to 3.53%.

“Fed Governor Waller is on the wires stating emphatically that the U.S. labor market is the furthest thing from being tight, wage growth is cooling which means that the supply-shortage narrative is overblown, and that all this leads to no second-round inflation impact from the tariff hikes. Just in case you’re wondering why the Treasury market is rallying,” said David Rosenberg, founder and president of Rosenberg Research & Associates.

Also within commodities, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) retreated from the $4,000 level, while silver (XAGUSD:CUR) has surpassed the $50 per ounce level. 

Wall Street's major indexes struggled to keep up momentum on Thursday and ended lower, as the U.S. Senate failed to pass two funding bills to end the government shutdown. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell did not comment on the economic outlook or monetary policy at a Fed event.
